message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 37
Recent Green phase: In processClaim, wrapped the summed desired payout in Math.floor (payouts round DOWN) before clamping with Math.min to policy.remainingCap.

IMPORTANT do NOT write experiment-done.txt — 7 todo tests remain (multiple same-type items each own deductible; reject when more damage entries of a type than insured; quote unknown-type error → non-zero exit + stderr; claim item-not-in-policy error; claim unknown-type error; claim negative-amount error; plus the CLI entry at src/cli.ts must be created and a quote-then-claim CLI test). Several of these are ERROR cases needing the CLI; the error tests will likely require runScenario (or the CLI) to throw on invalid input.

Refactor while keeping all tests green. processClaim is growing (find item, per-damage payout with clauses, sum, floor, clamp, mutate cap). Evaluate whether extracting a `desiredPayout(policy, damages)` helper clarifies it; evaluate naming; apply Four Rules of Simple Design; measure APP mass / McCabe / cognitive / ESLint before and after. Keep whichever scores better.

Run tests with `pnpm test`.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
37 passing, 7 todo. ESLint is clean. Now let me compute APP mass and McCabe for the recently-grown function, `processClaim`, which the Green phase just modified.

## PRE-Measurement: `processClaim` (the refactoring target)

**ESLint smells**: 0 (clean across the whole file)

**Cognitive complexity** (`processClaim`): 0 explicit branches in the function body. The nested `reduce` lambda adds nesting, but SonarJS reports nothing (under threshold).

**McCabe Cyclomatic Complexity** (`processClaim`):
- Start: 1
- No `if`/`case`/`&&`/`||`/`?:`/`for`/`while`/`catch` in the function body itself
- The `reduce` callback contains no decision points either

McCabe: **1**

**APP Mass** (`processClaim`), lines 161–171:
- Constants: `0` (in reduce) → 1
- Bindings: `policy`, `claim`, `desired`, `sum`, `damage`, `payout` → 6
- Invocations: `Math.floor`, `reduce`, `damagePayout`, `insuredItem`, `+` (sum), `Math.min`, `-=` arithmetic on remainingCap... counting the calls: `Math.floor`(1), `.reduce`(1), `damagePayout`(1), `insuredItem`(1), `+`(1), `Math.min`(1) → 6 invocations → 12
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: `reduce` → 1 → 5
- Assignments: `policy.remainingCap -= payout` → 1 → 6

Total Mass: `1·1 + 6·1 + 6·2 + 0·4 + 1·5 + 1·6` = `1 + 6 + 12 + 0 + 5 + 6` = **30**

Now the question posed: does extracting `desiredPayout(policy, damages)` clarify `processClaim`? The current `processClaim` mixes two concerns: (1) computing the desired payout (reduce + floor over damages with clause logic via `damagePayout`/`insuredItem`), and (2) clamping to the cap and mutating policy state. Extracting the computation isolates the pure calculation from the cap-mutation, revealing intent.

Let me compute the projected POST metrics for the extraction.

**Projected `desiredPayout(policy, damages)` helper:**
```ts
const desiredPayout = (policy: Policy, damages: Damage[]): number =>
  Math.floor(
    damages.reduce(
      (sum, damage) => sum + damagePayout(damage, insuredItem(policy, damage.itemType)),
      0,
    ),
  );
```
- Constants: `0` → 1
- Bindings: `policy`, `damages`, `sum`, `damage` → 4
- Invocations: `Math.floor`, `reduce`, `damagePayout`, `insuredItem`, `+` → 5 → 10
- Loops: `reduce` → 5
- Assignments: 0

Helper mass: `1 + 4 + 10 + 0 + 5 + 0` = **20**. McCabe: 1.

**Projected slimmed `processClaim`:**
```ts
const processClaim = (policy: Policy, claim: ClaimStep): ClaimResult => {
  const payout = Math.min(desiredPayout(policy, claim.incident.damages), policy.remainingCap);
  policy.remainingCap -= payout;
  return { payout, remainingCap: policy.remainingCap };
};
```
- Constants: 0
- Bindings: `policy`, `claim`, `payout` → 3
- Invocations: `Math.min`, `desiredPayout` → 2 → 4
- Loops: 0 (moved into helper)
- Assignments: `-=` → 1 → 6

Slimmed mass: `0 + 3 + 4 + 0 + 0 + 6` = **13**. McCabe: 1.

The extraction drops `processClaim` from mass 30 → 13 and separates the loop-heavy computation into a named, independently-testable `desiredPayout`. This serves Rule 2 (reveals intent): `processClaim` now reads as "compute desired payout, clamp to cap, record it." Per APP, even the combined mass (13 + 20 = 33 vs 30) rises slightly by the cost of one extra invocation — but Rule 2 trumps APP, and the clarity gain plus the independently-testable pure function justify it.

Let me apply this single refactoring.

content.text:
ESLint stays clean, all 37 tests still green. The refactoring is kept.

## Refactoring Complete

**Refactoring applied**: Extracted `desiredPayout(policy, damages)` from `processClaim`, separating the pure payout computation (reduce + floor over damages, with per-damage deductible/clause logic) from the cap-clamp-and-mutate concern.

**Naming evaluation**: `processClaim` still fits — it now reads cleanly as "compute desired payout, clamp to remaining cap, record it." The new helper `desiredPayout` names the previously-anonymous intermediate (`desired`) and makes the calculation independently testable.

**Pre/Post deltas (function `processClaim`)**:
- ESLint smells: 0 → 0 (clean before and after)
- Cognitive complexity: 0 → 0 (no branches either way)
- McCabe cyclomatic: 1 → 1 (no decision points moved)
- APP mass: 30 → 13 (the loop/floor computation moved into the helper)
- New helper `desiredPayout`: mass 20, McCabe 1

**Tests**: 37/37 passing.

**Simple Design rationale**: The combined mass rose marginally (30 → 33) due to one extra invocation (the `desiredPayout` call), but Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP minimization. `processClaim` is now half its former mass and expresses exactly three responsibilities at a glance, and the extracted calculation can be tested without touching cap-mutation. McCabe and cognitive were already at their floor (1 / 0), so no decision-flattening was available or needed.

Per the instructions, I am NOT writing `experiment-done.txt` — 7 todo tests remain (multiple same-type items each own deductible; reject when more damage entries of a type than insured; quote unknown-type error; claim item-not-in-policy error; claim unknown-type error; claim negative-amount error; plus the CLI entry at `src/cli.ts` and the quote-then-claim CLI test).

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_01-30-14_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
